data.title: CALL FOR PAPERS - Volume 2, Issue 2
data.body:
The Editorial Board invites original and unpublished research papers, articles, case comments, and book reviews for Volume 2, Issue 2 of our journal. There is no prescribed theme, and submissions on any contemporary or classical topic relevant to law, social sciences, policy, humanities, or allied disciplines are welcome.

Last Date for Submission:

15 June 2026

Submission Guidelines:

All manuscripts must strictly adhere to the Submission Guidelines provided on our official website. Submissions not conforming to the guidelines will not be considered for review.

How to Submit:

Authors may submit their manuscripts through the Submission Portal or via the official submission email as specified on the website.

Review Process:

Every submission will undergo a double-blind peer review. Acceptance will be communicated only after the completion of the review cycle.

Publication:

Selected papers will be published in Volume 2, Issue 2, scheduled for release as per the journal's publication calendar.

For details regarding formatting, structure, citation style, and other requirements, please refer to the Submission Guidelines available on the journal's website.

We look forward to receiving your valuable contributions.

-Editorial Board

data.topic: Sextortion: A continuing mode of cyber crime
data.abstract:
Sextortion, a form of cybercrime obvious by the exploitation of intimate pictures or videos for fiscal, sexual, or psychological advantage, has become an rising global threat in the digital age. This crime includes forcing victims into compliance-often by bullying to openly release flexible content if specific demands are not met. As wrongdoers exploit the suppression and reach provided by digital platforms, sextortion cases progressively target vulnerable individuals, including juveniles and young adults. The emotional influence on sufferers is thoughtful, ranging from apprehension and depression to extreme cases of suicide, underlining the urgent need for robust legal, technical, and edifying measures.

These abstract goals to discuss the skeletons of sextortion as a contemporary cybercrime, investigating into its psychological, legal, and technical dimensions. Furthermore, it highpoints the urgent need for a multi-disciplinary method that involves lawmakers, cybersecurity specialists, educators, and numerical platform providers to implement preventive measures and effective response campaigns. In hypothesis, sextortion underscores the obligation for a resilient framework to shield individuals from mistreatment in an increasingly digitalized the world.

Introduction
Sextortion, a term derived from "sex" and "extortion," refers to a troubling cybercrime that exploits the secrecy and dignity of individuals through intimidations of releasing sexually overt images or videos unless certain demands are met. These demands may range from fiscal payments to additional explicit factual or even coercing victims into unwanted sexual acts. As a fast growing form of online abuse, sextortion influences the ease and anonymity of digital communication to target individuals of all ages and backgrounds, with young people, particularly adolescents and young adults, amongst the most vulnerable. The pervasive nature of digital campaigns and social media platforms has further strengthened the risk, enabling perpetrators to manipulate victims from effectively anywhere in the world.
This introduction examines sextortion as an budding cybercrime with extensive implications for individual wellbeing, public policy, and digital ethics. By considerate its mechanisms and consequences, backers can work toward emerging preventive measures, improving legal frameworks, and supporting victims to generate a safer digital environment for all.
Modus Operandi
Sextortion outlines stereotypically follow a systematic outline that manipulates trust, anonymity, and digital accessibility to exploit victims. The modus operandi can contrast based on the strategies and expertise used by wrongdoers, but most sextortion crimes disclose through a series of mutual steps. The stages which illustrate how wrongdoers expand control over victims, exploit private information, and ultimately force them into compliance.
1.	Early Interaction and Training: Wrongdoers often initiate interaction with potential victims on social media, dating apps, or other online daises under the guise of friendship, idealistic interest, or professional networking. They stereotypically make bogus profiles to appear credible, sometimes copying familiar figures or fabricating entire facades. Through conversation and trust-building, the wrongdoer gains entree to personal information, which is often used as influence later in the system.
2.	Gathering of Penetrating Substantial: Once faith is recognized, the wrongdoer may persuade the victim to share intimate photos or videos, from time to time framing it as a mutual exchange. In other cases, they might hack into devices, or online accounts to gain private images without the victim's acquaintance. Some aggressors also use webcam hacking or malware to record private videotape without consensus.
3.	Intimidations and Bullying: Through penetrating material in hand, the wrongdoer initiates the extortion stage, threatening to publication the images or videos publicly or share them with the victim's networks, household, or workplace unless exact demands are met. These difficulties may include cash transfers, further overt content, or other forms of obedience. Wrongdoers often intensify their threats to increase psychological load on victims, leveraging their fear of social humiliation.
4.	Separation and Operation: Wrongdoers recurrently use terrorization strategies to separate victims, making them feel abandoned or blameworthy. This sensitive manipulation may include continuous threats or declarations that the demands will stop if the victim fulfils. Numerous victims, especially undeveloped people, experience intense guilt, which discourages them from seeking aid, further detaching them and making them more vulnerable to constant exploitation.
Through considerate the steps in this modus operandi, establishments, digital platforms, and educational organizations can work near creating pre-emptive measures, permitting potential victims, and emerging strategies to dislocate the typical sextortion cycle. Additionally, alertness programs that teach the public on these strategies can stimulate potential victims to diagnose initial signs of sextortion, pursue timely help, and alleviate harm.
Soft Target
In the background of sextortion, "soft targets" refer to individuals who are mainly vulnerable to this form of cybercrime due to explicit personal, social, or psychological factors. Thoughtful who these soft targets are and why they are inclined is essential in developing effective prevention strategies and support systems.
1.	Adolescents and Young Adults: Adolescents and young adults are communal bull's eye for sextortion due to their high levels of online activity and frequently partial awareness of secrecy risks. 
2.	Individuals Seeking Companionship: Those who are isolated and actively watching for relationships or companionship online are also recurrent soft targets.
3.	Marginalized Communities: Individuals from sidelined societies, counting LGBTQ+ individuals or those in conventional social circles, can be defenseless to sextortion due to the risk of social or family refusal. In the world or communities with robust social dishonors around sexuality, victims may be more effortlessly coerced because the consequences of exposure could be plain, potentially leading to snubbing or worse.
Prominent Instances
State of West Bengal v. Animesh Boxi, 2018 in this case, the naked videos that were sent to multiple persons were recorded by the girl herself on her phone. The accused had the footage and decided to share it in a fit of rage. The offender was punished by five years imprisonment and a fine of Rs. 9000 in this case. Hon'ble Judges was made decisions based on justice and fairness. This case was regarded to be India's first "revenge porn" conviction.
Caution to Be Taken
Averting sextortion requires watchfulness, accountable online behaviour, and proactive steps to shield personal privacy. These restraints apply to anyone who interrelates online, especially those who may be weaker to cyber manipulation. Here are some vital actions for preservation oneself against sextortion:
1.	Perimeter Sharing of Private and Intimate Content: Evade sharing overt images or videos, even with people you trust. 
2.	Validate Online Influences: Exercise caution when cooperating with new contacts online, especially on social media, dating app platforms. Verify individualities when conceivable, and be sceptical of profiles with little info, few influences, or new accounts-communal red flags for fake or malevolent profiles.
3.	Toughen Privacy Settings: Appraisal and adjust secrecy settings on all social media accounts, ensuring that private information is available only to reliable individuals. Boundary what outsiders or new acquaintances can see to decrease the risk of exposure to probable offenders.
4.	Be aware of Suspicious Links and Messages: Duck ticking on unknown links, particularly those sent through unswerving messages or emails, as these could contain malware designed to entree your device's camera, files, or personal information. Be careful of messages that appear too forward or ask for private information early on.
5.	Refuge Your Webcam When Not in Use: Some wrongdoers may use spyware to increase entree to a victim's webcam. To avert illegal recording, retain your webcam enclosed when not in use, or restrict it through your device's settings.
6.	Instruct Yourself on Cybersecurity Practices: Straightforward cybersecurity practices, such as diagnosing phishing efforts, using trustworthy security software program, and evading unsecured Wi-Fi networks, can go a extended way in stopping unauthorized access to strategies and accounts.
7.	Tale Suspicious Behaviour: If somebody begins to burden you for explicit gratified or money under threats, report the individual to the podium and block them immediately. Most social media and communication platforms have instruments for reporting unmannerly behaviour and will take action to prevent supplementary exploitation.
8.	Pursue Support and Report to Authorities: If targeted, recall that support is available. Victims should sensation sanctioned to report sextortion efforts to local authorities or cybercrime units without fear of judgment or retaliation. Many formations offer private counselling and care services specifically for cybercrime victims.
Captivating these defences can deliberately reduce the jeopardy of becoming a sextortion target. 
Prompt steps to be taken
Lodge A Complaint Against Sextortion 
It is necessary to file a complaint against sextortion. It is easy to make complaint. There are such steps -
Offline mode
1.	 File a formal report with the nearest cybercrime cell.
2.	 Share your personal details when you filed a complaint such as you name, phone number and address.
3.	If you are unable to make a complaint with the cyber cell, you may register an FIR with any police station. If they refuse your complaint, may appeal to the commissioner or judicial magistrate. 
Under section 154 of the CrPC it is mandatory to record the complaint.
Online mode
Go to the website Ministry of Home Affairs' National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal
1.	Click on the "File a Complaint".
2.	Click the "Accept".
3.	Click the option "Report cybercrime involving women/child" if your complaint is about a cybercrime involving a women or child. 
4.	Then click "Report".
5.	If your report is not related to this then click another option "Report other cybercrimes".
Provisions Related To Sextortion And Punishment For Sextortion In India
In India there is no explicit law that expressly deals with sextortion. But in Indian laws there are some provisions recognize one or the other aspects of sextortion. 
1.	Section. 354 of Indian Penal Code,1860 deals with assault or criminal force to a women with intent to outrage her modesty.
2.	Section. 354 A of Indian Penal Code, 1860 deals with sexual harassment and punishment for sexual harassment.
3.	Section. 375 of Indian Penal Code, 1860 deals with rape, done in different capacities, one of which is rape due to the abuse of authority and Section. 376 deals with punishment for rape.
4.	Section. 383 of Indian Penal Code, 1860 which deals with law against extortion.
5.	Section. 384 of Indian Penal Code, 1860 deals with punishment for extortion.
6.	Information Technology Act, 2000 deals with certain sexual offences, though it also does not cover the changing aspects of cyber sexual crime.
7.	The Shield of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012 deals with child sexual abuse.
Conclusion
Sextortion is becoming a common phenomenon in India. Sextortion is a serious and dangerous crime. Sextortion also involves blackmail in which the victim is threatened to share sexual photographs in order to extort money or sexual pleasure by sextortionists. The most prevalent form of sextortion is blackmail through social media. If anyone has been a victim of sextortion, he/she must report it immediately in nearly police station.
Citation
i.State of West Bengal v. Animesh Boxi, (2018)
ii.United States v. Rodger, (2020) 981 F.3d 1001 (9th Cir) 
Bibliography
i.	K D Gaur, INDIAN PENAL CODE (Universal LexisNexis, 7th edn., 2020).
ii.	Sneha Mahawar, 'How to take legal action against Sextortion' 5 June 2021, available at <https://blog.ipleaders.in/take-legal-action-sextortion/> (4 November, 2024)
iii.	'How to file a cyber complaint against online sextortion' 8 February 202, available at <https://www.onlinelegalindia.com/blogs/file-cyber-crime-compliant-online-in-india-against-online-sextortion> (4 November, 2024)
iv.	'Sextortion: An emerging crime into the gray area of law', available at <https://legalserviceindia.com/legal/article-6707-sextortion-an-emerging-crime-into-the-gray-area-of-law.html> (November, 2024)

data.topic: Science in the Dock: How Judges Navigate Conflicting Expert Testimonies
data.abstract:
The phrase "Science in the Dock" refers to a situation in which scientific discoveries or methods are put on trial, particularly in criminal trials. This occurs when there is an examination of forensic proof, an expert scientific witness, or any particular technique that a scientist used is questioned. 
The legal domain now has to deal with the competition of law and science. It tackles the nuanced realm where, as in both civil and criminal disputes, contested claims are negotiated into competing evidences or truths, and judgment is passed not by trained practitioners of scientific approaches. From forensic examination to litigation over climate change, the reliability of the so-called expert's claims can tip the scale toward justice or injustice, imprisonment or freedom, accountability or unaccountability for corporations.
The box marked 'court' is not optimal for a systematic conversation. In like manner to legal research, unlike other science disciplines that thrive on perpetual hypotheses, endless revisions, and ambiguous conclusions, 'science' in law lacks peer-reviewed scrutiny, only having check-listed 'proceedings' that demand either one or the other conclusion: guilty or innocent, liable or not to be considered innocent.
This paper explores into the mechanisms courts use to evaluate expert opinions, from the Daubert standard's criteria for reliability to the risks of "junk science" swaying juries. This also explore https://www.duplichecker.com/s the intersection of science and law, focusing on how judges navigate conflicting expert testimonies, the limitations of forensic science, and potential reforms to strengthen the judicial evaluation of scientific evidence.
data.body:
Introduction
The underpinning of the Indian judicial system rests on the maxim Ei incumbit probatio qui dicit, non qui negat the burden of proof lies on the one who asserts, not on the one who refutes. This replicates the assumption of goodness until proven guilty, wherein an individual is considered innocent. Forensic science considered as an essential part of criminal justice. 
Admissibility of Scientific Evidence in Criminal Trial
Admissible evidence refers to info presented in court that meets legal standards, meaning it cannot be dismissed based on trivial arguments. Such evidence must hold probative price that is, it must be appropriate of proving or disproving a fact in issue. 
To introduce scientific evidence in court, experts employ various test center and fact-finding techniques. An expert witness, usually somebody with specialized knowledge or experience, is called upon to explain and defend the methodology and detections behind the scientific evidence being offered. This expert's testimony can show a momentous role in determining the outcome of a trial, particularly when it worries questions of guilt or innocence. Forensic experts, in particular, often testify about their measures and conclusions during criminal proceedings.
In the dominion of forensic science, the judge's role is vital. The focus should not solely be on whether a new scientific theory is broadly accepted, but on whether the specific application of that theory in the case at hand meets the core evidentiary requirements: authenticity, relevance, and reliability .
Experts Testimonies Under the Indian Legal System
Generally, when a person is summoned to court for giving testimony as a witness, he is expected to state only facts and not expected to state only facts and not give opinion. It is the job of the court to verify the all evidence and form an opinion in the case. But there is an exception, the experts are considered as witnesses although they are not actually related to the case. The new evidence act is called Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam (BSA), 2023 which replaced Section 45 and retains the provision for expert opinion under Section 39. When the court needs an opinion in a subject which requires special knowledge, the court calls the person who is skilled with special knowledge and experiences in a particular area of subject because without their opinion the court cannot form a correct judgement. The opinion given by the person who is specialized with special knowledge which are not the jurisdiction of layman, is called an expert and the opinion given by them is known as expert opinion.
Example: The court is not sure about the fingerprint of the person 'Y'. That time the court calls a fingerprint expert to find out the same. This person will be known as an expert and the opinion given by that person is relevant and known as expert opinion. 
Shortly Discussed the Provisions of Expert Opinion Mentioned Under Indian Evidence Act, 1872
The longstanding Indian Evidence Act, 1872 described the expert opinion under the Section 45 to 51 . According to Section 45 a person will be considered as an expert when they have developed their time in learning a special branch of expertise and thus are specially skilled in the subject, which must include special knowledge, observation and practical experiences.
Experts is not judge and also their opinion are not obligatory on the court. Because of an expert opinion, the court does not become functus officio (having performed one's duty'). At the end, the final opinion has to be given by the court. The courts need an expert to form an opinion: 
1. Foreign law: When a case involves the interpretation of foreign law, the court may rely on the opinion of an expert in that legal system. The expert must be someone familiar with the laws of the foreign country. Because the court itself cannot take judicial notice of foreign law and depends on expert testimony.
2. Science and Art: The word 'science and Art' are to be broadly constructed. The term 'science' and 'is not limited to fine arts, but having its original senses of handicraft, trade, profession and skill in work. 
3. Identity of finger impression: Fingerprint evidence is well thought-out highly consistent in criminal cases for establishing identity.
4. Electronic evidence: When information is conveyed or stored in a computer system and the court requires expert aid to understand or assess it, the matter may be referred to an Examiner of Electronic Evidence, who is well-thought-out an expert of such cases.
Other expert evidence areas include fingerprints, brain mapping, polygraphs, DNA, ballistics and many others. 
Facts bearing upon opinion of experts: Section 46 of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872 provides that when an expert gives an opinion on a relevant matter, any facts that either support or contradict that opinion also become relevant in court proceedings.
Illustration: If it is necessary to determine whether A has been poisoned, then the fact that A shows symptoms which experts confirm or deny as being consistent with that specific poison becomes relevant to the case.
Value of Expert Evidence Under Other Laws
Scientific evidence is admissible in India. 
A.	The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973
Most important acknowledged aspect of Forensic Science is DNA and it's testing. There is no official legislation passed w.r.t. to DNA testing in India , but Sec 53 and 54 of Cr.P.C deals with examination of alleged person by a certified medical practitioner on reasonable grounds of inquest. The Indian government and the Law Commission also became aware of the issue, and the Indian Parliamentary Affairs Board has established an advisory committee to provide a complete report on all aspects of DNA testing. 
Section 53A authorizes investigating officers to collect DNA samples from individuals accused of rape, with the assistance of registered medical practitioners.
B.	The Identification of Prisoners Act, 1920
This act allows investigating officers to collect fingerprint and footprint impressions from suspects.
Section 5 specifically allows a magistrate to order a person to be measured or photographed for investigation purposes. If satisfied that it is necessary for an investigation or legal proceeding under the CrPC, the magistrate can issue such an order, requiring the individual to appear at a specified time and place for measurements or photographs to be taken by a police officer.
However, only a First-Class Magistrate is authorized to issue orders for photography. 
The Information Technology Act, 2000
This act introduced the concept of electronic evidence into Indian law.
Through Section 92 of the IT Act, the Indian Evidence Act was amended to include "electronic records" within the definition of evidence, thus making digital evidence legally admissible.
While the IT Act does not explicitly define or address "expert opinion" as the Indian Evidence Act does, it implies the need for expert involvement in areas requiring technical expertise.
Section 79A of the Information Technology Act says that The Central Government may notify any department, body or agency of the government as an Examiner of Electronic Evidence. Their role is to provide expert opinion on electronic evidence in any proceeding before a court or authority. This provision gives legal recognition to electronic forensics experts .
Judges Navigate the Expert Testimony
A.	Appraising the Credentials of the Expert
The Evidence Act of the Expert System 45 Judiciary relates to laws within relationships where an expert's testimony is necessary in areas that contain specialized knowledge, such as science, handwriting, foreign law, or medicine. There is no testimony without an examination quite like the one in focus here, that of an expert's qualifications. In simple terms, pre-exam requirements, curriculum vitae, job history, publications, and the most important in this context, ancillary.
If they are conflicting, the locus of authority, or the court in our case relies on the one expert who has the most appropriate education, specialization, or even years working concerning the subject they argue on. To illustrate, the trained and experienced forensic scientist will overpower the general practitioner on the stand when the case's evidence is too much forward forensic.
B.	Determining the Method Employed and Acceptance
 After all the necessary qualifications have been approved the next in line is the methodology. In this case the opinion regurgitated should be that of one whose methods is known to be credible and verifiable scientifically, not rely on made up methods. Opinions based on conjectures or not prevalent practices in a particular field - the court will not accept the opinion.
In the case State of H.P. v. Jai Lal,  the supreme court ruled in this matter that the expert opinions must be drawn from methods proven scientifically. Mixing methods or on approaches, that draws no boundaries, results in a lack of appropriate or trustworthy evidence. 
C.	Coherence and Verification with Other Facts
Expert testimony, in isolation, lacks meaningful evidential value. This type of testimony must also be supported by other facts and pieces of evidence related to the case at hand.An expert's conclusion that contradicts proven facts or stands unsupported by any corroborative material may be rejected.
In the case Murari Lal v. State of M.P,  the court ruled that expert evidence is merely advisory and not conclusive. It must be evaluated along with other evidence.
In the case Ramesh Chandra Agrawal v. Regency Hospital Ltd.,  The case law suggests that the court is under no obligation to accept an expert opinion which is contrary to established facts or evidence.  
Thus, the expert's input is important, but the judgment is based on the overall evidence presented.  
D.	Cross Examination (Section 146)
As stated in Section 146, the party in opposition also has the right to cross-examine the expert. This is the most important moment in which the credibility of the expert can be verified. 
This procedure guarantees that testimony provided by an expert will not be taken as truth blindly and will assist the court in verifying that the expert has acted professionally and rationally.  
E.	Expert by the Court on Its Own Initiative (Section 165)
 In very sophisticated or advanced technical matters, the court might find it hard to deal with conflicting expert opinions. In such cases, the judge is allowed under Section 165 to appoint a specific individual whose expertise is not limited to the sides involved in the dispute to aid the court.
This independent expert does not belong to either side and must give an impartial and credible opinion based on factual data. The judge may also interrogate this expert directly to clarify technical points.
Constitutionality And Admissibility of Expert Testimony
Expert evidence is admissible in court only when the expert is examined as a witness. For such evidence to be considered valid, the expert must clearly explain the basis of their opinion and be available for cross-examination by the opposing party. Without this, the opinion is not admissible.
Legal provisions for the examination of experts are laid out in various statutes. Under Section 293 of the Criminal Procedure Code reports submitted by government scientific experts are admissible as evidence in inquiries, trials, or other judicial proceedings. If the expert is unable to appear in person, the court may allow a qualified and knowledgeable officer from the expert's team, who is familiar with the analysis and findings, to appear on their behalf.
Problems With Their Reforms and Solutions
Problems 
A.	The judge's inability to apply scientific training to cases
The overwhelming of judges are trained lawyers and have no technological education. Consequently, they lack the requisite skills to properly evaluate the scientific data or the highly sophisticated methodologies which are entwined with the scientific components of trials. Understanding science to this level does not exist for many judges and therefore they are forced to make decisions based on clever cross-examinations and pathetic diatribes which do not demarcate the lies from the reality in the information given by the so-called experts.  
B.	Determining whose judgment is credible
The court is frequently left to determine which argument coming from expert testimony is the most credible when several experts give contradicting testimonies. Analyzing pieces of evidence in these testimonies is both challenging and unwieldy because of how technical the arguments are. Judges and jurors without deep knowledge of the science underlying the testimony may be faced with superficial indicators like assertiveness, educational qualifications, and even mannerisms instead of actual sound arguments.
C.	Subjectivity and prejudice
Despite the impartial stance expert witnesses are expected to adopt, personal biases, financial gain, or affiliations can easily alter the quality of presented evidence. The objectivity achieved by experts may be severely distorted under the undue influence of one party hiring an expert known to actively spin the outcome into a certain interpretation.
D.	Different Levels of Competency
There is no single criterion by which all experts can be evaluated. Courts may receive opinions from a spouse with varying competence and, without proper resources, they might fail to identify the difference between a subject-area resource and an inadequate dabbling non-expert. This imbalance leads to the testimony being assigned different values which is not proportional to the weight that should be given.  
E.	Complicated Nature of the Evidence
Evidence oftentimes incorporates expert analysis which can be based on highly complex scientific methodologies, sophisticated statistical computations, or cutting-edge jargon. This expert capsule may be too much for a member of jury to comprehend, or even a judge for that matter, which will result in the trimming down of the oversimplified version of sophisticated problems. The critical subtleties which are fundamental to the correct resolution of the case are omitted along with the translation. 
F.	Boundaries of Scope of Expert Testimony
Disputes and controversies within the law resulting from a definite legal boundary which is going to be set for the range of expert evidence can at times also occur. Every now and then, interdisciplinary experts tend to overreach their boundaries and provide an irrelevant opinion concerning the legal question and issue which is ideally supposed to be handled by the court. It is equally as important for the judge to take notice of, and make use of, the expert evidence presented while ensuring that he or she does not become deprived of the use of judicial sovereignty.
G.	Issues with Cross-Examinations
The highly IT related reasoning given by the expert may prove to be impossible to cross-examine for most attorneys. Having a lack of broad understanding may inhibit the attorney's ability to maximize exploitation of the expert's analysis. Such a situation may arise that expert's statement which is actually incorrect goes unchallenged throughout legal scrutiny.
H.	Lack of Adaptability
Rapid progress observed in science and technology does not fully integrate into the legal framework. Evidence by the expert may still be considered acceptable or reject without relying on more up-to-date information evidence standards. For example, some forensic techniques that were previously accepted as accurate measures may be discredited later as they had become obsolete, but the judiciary is unable to move past the change in acceptability.
I.	Increase of Work Due to Disagreement
In the event that there are highly, strongly differing views shared by different experts, it forces courts to bring in other experts or other hearings which consume time and litigation funding. This creates a scenario where justice becomes inaccessible and financial obligations become amplified for the parties within the dispute.
Reforms & Solutions
A.	Strengthening Credibility Assessment of Expert Witnesses
1."Daubert Standard" Adoption: Currently, Indian courts are guided by Section 45 of the Indian Evidence Act (IEA), 1872 which does not provide adequate guidance on expert evidence evaluation. It is suggested that a structured admissibility test such as the Daubert standard (from the USA) which offers criteria to measure the scientific validity of an expert's evidence be considered. The court considers the following standards:
If a theory or method is capable of being tested and has been tested, has it also been evaluated through peer review. Such as: What are the established or possible margins of error associated with it. And also the existence and maintenance of various standards. Implementing this logic in India, be it through judicial activism or statutory reform, will enable us to administer proper control in the acceptance of expert testimony.
2. Advisors from the Scientific Field or Specialized Science Courts: The diverse technical and scientific evidence may surpass the training judges obtain. Some jurisdictions (France and Germany) have adopted science courts, or have technical advisors bridge this cap. This could be useful in India by:
Setting up specialized science benches in High Courts, or tribunal units for forensics, medicine, and cyber security.
3. Conflict of Interests that Must Be Disclosed: In order to maintain biased credibility-Affiliation, financial interest, or other dealings with the party must be disclosed by experts, and such disclosure must be mandatory. 
In relation to the above, courts can consider disallowing or removing the designation of an expert if his/her's bio is marked with a significant conflict of interest; it can be done through section 146 (cross examination) of IE Act, and bias can be brought out in the open which is the motive in question. 
B.	Objectivity and Bias Reduction
1. Experts Appointed by the Court who Remain Neutral: A judge is recommended to exercise powers within Indian Evidence Act, which empowers the judge to ask questions to uncover the truth under Section 165 of the and the Civil Procedure Code pertains to appointing commissions for local investigations under Order 26, Rule 9.
And appoint such experts as they deem fit to be independent. This type of selection neutralizes partisanship resulting from adversarial engagement or the use of partisan professionals as well as objectivity as it was supported in State of H.P. v. Jai Lal . 
2. More Strict Ethical Standards for Experts: There is no comprehensive code of expert witnesses conduct in place for India. It would be the responsibility of the Bar Council of India or the Law Commission to formulate. 
Ethical norms that are set should also cover being unbiased and disclosure admitting non-compliance can be non-evidentiary and falsifying evidence violations indeed can be endorsing accepting section 193 IPC (perjury). There is judicial Marked UK Civil Procedure rules Part 35. Such rules require adorning court assistance for party supporting rather than experts.
C.	Ensuring Uniform Expertise Standards
1. Accreditation of Experts: Only certified individuals, like those from Forensic Science Laboratories, should be allowed to give expert testimony. Courts are to undertake a credential check to ensure that the evidence given is within the crediential scope of trained and experienced individuals rather than unqualified and pseudoscience dabblers.
2. Pre-Trial Voir Dire (examination of the expert witnesses preliminarily): Borrowed from common law, courts should also undertake voir dire to assess- Qualifications, Methodologies and Relevance of the proposed expert testimony. This avoids unqualified or unreliable testimony from being led at later stages in the proceedings.
D.	The Strategy of Dealing with Complex Evidence for the Courts and the Juries 
1. Use of Court Experts (Amicus Curiae in Technical Cases): Judges are permitted to appoint the technical expert as amicus curiae in difficult cases, as adviced in Vikramjit Singh v. State of M.P . Further, Judicial academies ought to provide literacy training on forensics, digital evidence, including AI.
2. Bringing Clarity into Comprehension: Experts should provide- A technical report, and a summary of such report in a simple language.
This two-fold approach will help the judges and even lawyers analyze and appreciate the evidence.
E.	Scope Clarification for Expert Opinion Details  
Apply "Relevance" & "Helpful" Criteria Literally: Expert opinion should be heard and accepted only when strictly necessary (per Section 45, IEA). Speculative opinions should be rejected as held in Ramesh Chandra Agrawal v. Regency Hospital.  
F.	Improving Efficiency of Expert Cross Examination  
1. Disclosure of Expert Reports Before Trial: Foster exchange of reports before trial (under Order 18, CPC) so that lawyers prepare effective cross-examinations.  
2. Training Lawyers on Technical Cross Examination: Law schools, including NALSAR, ILI, and Bar Associations, should develop courses on advanced forensic science and technical evidence, including mock trials where advanced expert cross-examinations are emphasized.  
G.	Keeping up with developments 
1. Advanced Precedent in Legal Frameworks: New AI, neuroscience, and digital forensics warrant agile approaches within the law. 
2. New Dynamic Precedent: Courts need to adopt international jurisprudence practices (consideration of DNA profiling standards in Selvi v State of Karnataka ) to strengthen and modernize India's evidential framework.
H.	Preventing Delays Due to Conflicting Opinions
To avoid a "battle of experts," courts should limit the number of expert testimonies unless necessary. 
Early Case Management: Judges should identify technical disputes early and appoint neutral experts in the pre-trial phase, ensuring focused and timely resolution of scientific issues. 
Empirical Insights with Clear Guidance
Ramesh Chandra Agrawal v. Regency Hospital Ltd & Ors :  In this landmark case, the court comprehensively examined the role and definition of an expert. It held that and expert must be devotes their time and study to a specific branch of knowledge. Such expertise may be acquired through practice, observation, or careful study. Importantly, Indian legal system does not power the experts to work as Judge or jury.
An expert is not considered a witness of fact like other witnesses; rather, their testimony is advisory in nature. Their responsibility is to provide the judge with the scientific or technical criteria necessary to assess the validity of conclusions presented. Judge independently evaluate the evidence using those criteria. The court also emphasized that no expert can assert with absolute certainty that their opinion is infallible.
Malay Kumar Ganguly v. Dr. Sukumar Mukherjee : In this case, the court held that it is not obligated to accept expert evidence as conclusive, since such evidence is largely advisory in nature. The court retains full authority to independently assess the expert opinions presented by both parties. It must do so with caution, considering relevant legal principles and precedents related to the subject matter of the expert's testimony.
Bant Singh v. Niranjan Singh :  In this case, the dispute arose over a property suit in which the plaintiff claimed that the defendant was his sister. The opinion of his brother-in-law who was 80 years old and has good knowledge of his family members was held as the relevant evidence.
A.	Supreme Court should issue binding precedents on when expert evidence is conclusive v. corroborative: 
State of Kerala v. Mahesh  : Expert opinion alone cannot convict without supporting evidence.
Gade Lakshmi Mangaraju v/s. State Of Andhra Pradesh : This case clarified that the absence of fingerprint evidence does not definitively prove that a person was not present at the scene.
State of Himachal Pradesh Vs. Jai Lal and Others : In this case, the court underscored the importance of cross-examining expert witnesses, alongside their responsibility to provide expert opinions.
Prahlad Saran Gupta v Bar Council of India : This case emphasised that there is no need for additional confirmation once an opinion from a handwriting expert is provided. This case discussed the weight and importance attributed to expert opinions, particularly in matters related to signatures and handwriting.
B.	Other Important Observations
Gautam Kundu v. State of West Bengal : In this case, it was established that Indian courts cannot order blood tests routinely or without proper justification. Such requests should not be allowed as a means of conducting a general or unfocused inquiry. There must be a strong prima facie case, and the court must carefully consider the potential consequences before directing a blood test.
Sharda v. Dharmpal : The court held that a matrimonial court has the authority to order medical tests, including DNA tests, but this power should be exercised only when the applicant presents robust prima facie case which must be reinforced by sufficient evidence. 
Conclusion
Experts' opinion plays a vital role in the Indian judicial scheme. They help understand and clarify complex issues which requires special knowledge. 
The are rules of prudence ensuring that an expert's opinion is reliable, such as an expert's testimony in divorce or bigamy cases having strong evidentiary support and the expert having other pieces of evidence to back their claim.  
The decision in S. Gopala Reddy vs State of A.P.  demonstrates these precautionary measures. "While Expert's Opinions are indeed helpful, they should be accompanied by other forms of evidence in order for the legal system to remain untainted and reliable."  
As in any case, the burden of proof (Section 101), and the phrasing "beyond reasonable doubt" followed in criminal cases, and "preponderance of probabilities" for civil cases determines the ultimate judgement.
Case Laws
1.	Daubert v. Merrell Dow Pharmaceuticals, Inc., 509 U.S. 579 (1993).
2.	State of Himachal Pradesh vs. Jai Lal and Others, Supreme Court of India.
3.	Ramesh Chandra Agrawal v. Regency Hospital Ltd & Ors., Supreme Court of India.
4.	Malay Kumar Ganguly v. Dr. Sukumar Mukherjee, Supreme Court of India.
5.	Bant Singh v. Niranjan Singh, Supreme Court of India.
6.	State of Kerala v. Mahesh, Supreme Court of India.
7.	Gade Lakshmi Mangaraju v. State of Andhra Pradesh, Supreme Court of India.
8.	Prahlad Saran Gupta v. Bar Council of India, Supreme Court of India.
9.	Gautam Kundu v. State of West Bengal, Supreme Court of India.
10.	Sharda v. Dharmpal, Supreme Court of India.
Bibliography
1.	Indian Evidence Act, 1872.
2.	The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973.
3.	Information Technology Act, 2000.
4.	The Identification of Prisoners Act, 1920.

data.topic: Sexual Harassment at the Workplace: Analysing the Implementation of the POSH Act, 2013
data.abstract: This paper contains detailed legislation on the issues of sexual harassment at workplace known as the POSH Act. In 2013, the government also enacted the Criminal Law (Amendment) Act, 2013, that penalised various types of sexual harassment. The paper further talks about the recommendations aimed to improve institutional accountability, ensuring a culture of zero tolerance, and delivering access to justice for victims. In doing so, it contributes to the broader discourse on workplace safety, gender equity, and the realization of women's constitutional rights in India.
data.body:
1.	Introduction
In 1992, in Rajasthan there was a Dalit social worker name Bhanwari Devi, who was gang-raped by some upper-caste men as the worker opposed child marriage. Despite the brutality of the offence all the accused were acquitted and they have been given minimal punishment. The state and her employer did not took any responsibility.
Due to public outrage a Public Interest Litigation was filed in the Supreme Court by women's rights activists, that results in the landmark judgment of Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan 1997. The Supreme Court, acknowledged the legal emptiness and issued the Vishaka Guidelines that makes it mandatory for all the employers to protect female employees from sexual harassment.
2.	Key Provisions of the Act
I.	Sexual Harassment
The definition includes sexual favours, physical advances, sexually colored remarks, showing pornography covering situations that involves promises or threats disturbing employment, interfering with work that harms a woman's dignity and safety. 
II.	Applicability and Scope
This Act applies throughout India and protects any woman irrespective of their age or employment status facing sexual harassment at a workplace covering both organized and unorganized sectors. 
III.	Employee
The Act also provides the definition of an 'employee' which include anyone working whether permanent, temporary including persons engaged directly or by an agent covering those working with or without the knowledge of principal employer.
IV.	Workplace
This Act further gives a definition of "workplace" that includes both the primary office and any location where the employee worked in the course of employment.
V.	Complaints Committee
A. Internal Committee (IC): In Every organization 10 or more employees are required to form an Internal Committee at each office or branch for addressing complaints of sexual harassment. Penalties can be imposed in case of Failure to form this committee.
B. Local Committee (LC): This Act further provided provisions to establish a LC to deal with the complaints of sexual harassment at the workplaces. It has less than 10 employees. 
3.	Implementation and effectiveness of the Act
Its implementation depends on various industries. Large companies and sectors such IT and corporate they usually comply with the provisions, but in case of insecurity jobs they sometimes discourages reporting. Sectors such as hospitality they also struggles for training and complaints. 
4.	Other Laws Pertaining to Workplace Sexual Harassment
I.	The Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act, 1946 
It mandates employers to define and publish conditions of employment, which includes work hours, wages, leave, and termination. 
II.	Indian Penal Code, 1860  
The act of Sexual harassment is also considered as an offence under the Indian Penal code. Various sections including 354, 354A, 354B, 354C, 354D, and 509 deals with sexual harassment and provides punishments.
III.	Companies Act, 2013
In 2018, the Companies (Accounts) Rules was amended by the Ministry of Corporate Affairs, which requires companies to disclose their compliance with the Act. In case of failure to include this disclosure may results in a fine starting from INR 50,000 to INR 25,00,000, and the officers of the company may face 3 years imprisonment, a fine, or both.
5.	The Vishaka Judgement
In 1992, in Rajasthan there was a Dalit social worker name Bhanwari Devi, who was gang-raped by some upper-caste men as the worker opposed child marriage. Despite the brutality of the offence all the accused were acquitted and they have been given minimal punishment. The state and her employer did not took any responsibility.
Due to public outrage a Public Interest Litigation was filed in the Supreme Court by women's rights activists, that results in the landmark judgment of Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan 1997. The Supreme Court, acknowledged the legal emptiness and issued the Vishaka Guidelines that makes it mandatory for all the employers to protect female employees from sexual harassment.
This horrific incident showing the severe dangers faced by female employee, especially those in vulnerable and ordinary roles, and highlighted the urgent requirement to formally protect women against sexual harassment at the workplace. This landmark pronouncement brought national attention to the prevalent issues of female employees at workplace.
6.	Post Vishaka Case laws
I.	Apparel Export Promotion Council v. A.K Chopra
In this case the Court made a important development in interpreting sexual harassment by upholding that physical contact was not a essential element for behaviour to establish sexual harassment. It further clarified that sexual harassment involves a broader area of conduct, including unwelcome sexual favours, sexual advances, and other spoken or physical actions with sexual hints whether explicit or implicit. 
II.	Medha Kotwal Lele & Ors. v. Union of India & Ors
This case recognises the serious concerns brought, the Supreme Court considered the letter as a writ petition taking suo motu cognizance of the matter. 
7.	Success Stories of the POSH Act's Implementation
I.	Corporate Sector - Tata Consultancy Services (TCS)
TCS has effectively executed the POSH Act, it established ICC focusing on gender sensitivity and confidentiality. In many instances, TCS investigated complaints filed by female employee against her senior manage. With strong training and awareness programs it has contributed to build trust in the ICC, ensuring prompt action, transparency, in addressing workplace harassment issues.
II.	Educational Sector - Jawaharlal Nehru University (JNU), Delhi
JNU has also applied the Act, safeguarding a strong obligation to gender impartiality. In a case, a complaint was filed by a student against a professor leading to an impartial investigation that resulted in the removal of that professor. JNU's focus on making an inclusive campus environment where students are encouraged to report harassment without fear and hesitation.
III.	Government Sector - Indian Railways
Indian Railways has played an important role in implementing the Act. In one such cases, an employee initiated complaint of harassment that led to a fast investigation and punitive action. 
8.	Challenges in addressing the issue
I.	Delays in Investigation 
One important challenge is the delay in investigating the complaints of harassment, resulting in lengthy suffering for the victims and weakens the effectiveness of the Act.
II.	Lack of Awareness 
Another significant issue is the inadequate awareness of the Act in small businesses and the informal sector, including lack of strong policies, sufficient training, and infrastructure to deal with such complaints that led to the destruction of the grievance, and also leaving the worker without any remedy. 
III.	Ineffectiveness of ICCs in Public Sector Institutions 
Due delays and inefficiencies within public sector organizations, it leads to complex and lengthy procedures, preventing prompt justice for victims. 
IV.	Retaliation and Social Stigma 
Further the social and cultural biases and the fear of stigma within some workplaces can prevent women from initiating complaints despite legal mechanisms in place. Even when effective procedures exist, the original social dynamics can often defeat the implementation of the Act.
V.	Inadequate Training and Awareness
Another significant challenge in implementing the Act effectively is the lack of proper training for both employees and management. Many employees are not aware of their rights and the procedures laid down in the Act. The members of the Internal Complaints Committees also may not have the essential skills for handling such sensitive cases effectively. 
9.	Loopholes
I. Ambiguities in the Definition of Sexual Harassment:
One of the main loopholes in the Act is the vague and inadequate definition of sexual harassment. A more clear definition is required to reduce the chances of misunderstanding and ensure consistency in implementation.
II. Inadequate Coverage of Non-Traditional Workspaces:
Another loopholes is the Act mainly focuses on outdated office environments, ignoring the growing number of updated workspaces, including remote work setups etc. As the form of work continues to change, the Act has to address these new work environments more efficiently and cover protections to persons working outside of the office settings.
III. Insufficient Provision for Third-Party Harassment:
One significant loopholes of this Act is that the Act holds employers responsible for stopping and addressing sexual harassment. But the Act does not mentioned anywhere such instances where harassment can be committed by third parties also, including clients, customers, or vendors. Therefore the Act needs to cover the broad range to include provisions that hold third parties liable and accountable for harassment which occurs in the workplace.
10.	Suggestions and Recommendations
I.	Expanding the Definition of Harassment to Include Digital Forms
A vital step in updating the Act is clearly recognizing various instances of digital harassment including online stalking, cyberbullying, and inappropriate behaviour in virtual spaces that can arise by social media, emails, video meetings and text messages. Therefore the Act must explicitly define virtual misconduct, safeguarding the forms of harassment are dealt with the same gravity as physical cases.
II.	Establishing Clear Guidelines for Digital Communication
For preventing virtual harassment, organizations should incorporate explicit policies for virtual interactions. Companies must provide framework as to what is to be considered as inappropriate digital behaviour. Moreover, precise codes of conduct should be laid down in case of video conferencing, internal chat platforms and email communication that will help employees to understand the limitations of professional and respectful behaviour.
III.	Strengthening Reporting Mechanisms for Virtual Harassment
Furthermore the Act should lay down convenient and safe reporting systems for those digital harassment. Employers must add online complaint portals within its management systems, to allow employees to make complaints of virtual misconduct without any hassle. Additionally for the encouragement of reporting, these platforms should ensure secrecy, privacy, and protection against retribution that will foster a safer and apparent workplace culture.
11.	Conclusion
From the above discussion we understood that this Act marks a noteworthy step in dealing with the issues of sexual harassment at workplace in India. Increasing awareness and training programs can help to cover all workplaces irrespective of its size or location. Likewise, proper legal mechanisms and quick resolve of complaints are important in building trust in the workplace. Thus the Act is considered as an important instrument to protect the rights of employees. Furthermore its achievement is subject to constant cooperation between employees and the concerned management in ensuring a truly protected and comprehensive work environment.
12.	Bibliography
1.	Nishith Desai Associates, "Prevention of Sexual Harassment at the Workplace (POSH)" 5-14 (2023)
2.	M. Krishnaveni and S.P Nandhini, "ANALYSIS and MECHANISM of PoSH ACT" 10 International Advanced Research Journal in Science, Engineering and Technology 309 (2023).
3.	Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan, AIR 1997 SC 3011
4.	Apparel Export Promotion Council v. A.K. Chopra, AIR 1999 SC 625
5.	Medha Kotwal Lele v. U.O.I., AIRONLINE 2012 SC 632
6.	Rehana Iqbal Imani, "An In-Depth Analysis of the Three Major Elements of the POSH Act: Prohibition of Sexual Harassment, Internal Complaints Committee, and Punishments for Violations" LegalOnus Law Journal (2024).
7.	Narendra Narayan Sharma and Dr. Shilpa Seth, "Critical Analysis of Loopholes in the Prevention of Sexual Harassment (POSH) Act: A Comprehensive Examination" 11 Journal of Scientific and Engineering Research 262 (2024).

data.keywords: POSH Act 2013, workplace sexual harassment laws, gender equality in India, institutional accountability reforms, women's rights and workplace safety

data.topic: Copyright law on Musical and Cinematograph film: A Supreme Court perspective
data.abstract: This paper contains how the Supreme Court has dealt with crucial issues, such as rights of lyricists and composers. In common practice, the cinematograph film is made of literary, dramatic and artistic work.. In India the owners of musical works and cinematograph films in India faces many challenges including ownership issues where a producer infringe the rights of composers and the lyricist. Sometimes the owners are not able to enforce their rights as it is complex. Further due to globalisation and piracy there arises piracy and unauthorized distribution of works.
data.body:
1. What is protected
Copyright protection extends to a wide range of works, including literary Works (Books, articles, poems, scripts, computer programs), Dramatic Works (Plays, choreographic works, etc.), Musical Works (Compositions with or without lyrics), Artistic Works (Paintings, sculptures, drawings, photographs, architectural designs, etc.), Cinematograph Films (Movies, videos, etc.), Sound Recordings.
2. What Rights Does Copyright Grant
Copyright gives the creator the rights of  reproducting, distributing,  adaptating, Communicating to the Public and translation.
3. Who Owns the Copyright?
If a work is commissioned, the person commissioning the work may own the copyright (depending on the agreement).
4. Copyright Registration
Registration is not mandatory but it is recommended. Registration provides legal evidence of ownership and can be helpful in case of infringement.
5. Copyright Law on Musical Works & Cinematograph Film 
The followings are fall under the scope of musical work:
A. Remix: The term "remix" has not been defined in the Act. It is a new version of a piece of music which has been created by putting together the individual instrumental and vocal parts in a different way. 
B. Song: Song is words sunt to tune. Song is not one type of work. It is a construction of different works. It is a formation of works and thus, copyright does not subsist. 
In common practice, the cinematograph film is made of literary, dramatic and artistic work. The followings fall under the scope of cinematograph film:
A. Motion picture 
b. Video, CD ROM, DVD captured by a closed circuit TV camera: The recording embodied in a video or a CD-ROM or a DVD or captured by a closed circuit TV camera has copyright just as much as if it was recorded on the traditional translucent film.
6. Author and Ownership
In general, the person who creates the work is the author of the work. The author of a piece of music is its composer. The Copyright Act by its clause (d) of section 2 gives the meaning of the term "author". The joint authors of a film are the producer and the principal director unless the producer and principal director are one and the same. 
7. Term of Protection 
The Indian Copyright Act, 1957, provides a specific term of protection for musical works. This term is crucial as it determines how long the copyright holder (or their successors) can exercise exclusive rights over their creation. 
The term of copyright protection for cinematograph films is generally the same as that for other works, which is the lifetime of the author plus 60 years. However, for cinematograph films, the copyright term is calculated from the date of publication of the film.  
8. Economic rights  
A. Reproduction right (copying): the Reproduction right is also known as copyright. 
B. Giving on hire of offer for hire (hiring right): Section 14 (d) (e) of the Act facilitate the owner's exclusive right in respect of giving on hire or offering hire the copy of a cinematograph film.
9. Moral rights  
A. Right of paternity: the creator of a work possesses the right to be identified as its author, this is the basic principle of the paternity right. 
B. Right of integrity: it means that an author possesses right to prevent distortion, mutilation, modification or other act in relation to the said work which would be prejudicial to his honour or reputation. This right protects the reputation of the author, which again also has its value for users of the work.
10. Infringement
According to section 51 of the Act, states that when any person, without a licence granted by the copyright or the Registrar of the Copyrights or in contravention of the conditions of a licence so granted or of any condition imposed by a competent authority under the Act reproduces the work in a material form, issues copies, makes film, store the work, performs the work shall constitute infringement.
11. Remedies
A. Civil remedies: it includes Injunction for restraining infringement or violation of copyright, damages in the form of monetary amount, account of profit, the profits to be measured by account which has been misappropriated by defendant for sale of infringed copies, and delivery up the infringing copies, the infringing copies can be claimed by the owner of copyright against infringer.
B. Criminal remedies: criminal remedies include imprisonment and imposition of fine, seizure of infringing copies and restoration of seized copies of a work or plates to the person with interest. 
12. Supreme Court Cases
I. Indian Performing Rights Society Ltd. v. Eastern India Motion Pictures Association 
The court's decision awarded IPRS with the rights to fee collection and royalties for the public performances of the works embedded in the films and the music in the films. Section 13(4) of the Copyright Act, 1957, treats cinematograph films as an independent and unique class of work and declares that copyright will subsist in them throughout the territory of India. 
II. R. G. Anand v. Deluxe Films 
The Supreme Court saw fit to dismiss the appeal as the two lower courts had on record that there was no breach of the copyright. The court also observed that as much as there is no need for a breach in copyright to be a 'hash' of the original work, a resemblance that is substantial enough will do. The court, however, remarked that with respect to stage plays, the frame of film works, proving piracy is challenging. 
13. The Importance of Registering Copyright
A. Legal Recognition: registration creates formal acknowledgment of rights of the owner which assists in the enforcement of provided rights in case of violations. 
B. Exclusive Rights: the registered copyrighted work is owned by the author with complete control to exploit the work commercially in any manner. This also allows the author to grant licenses for public performance, reproduction, and distribution which can result in great financial gain.
C. Protection against Infringement: registration enables the work owner to sue for the breach of their work at any time. This includes the rights to the work and to sue the infringer which usually stops others from carrying out the same illegal actions.
14. Developments 
A. Parliament Passes Cinematograph (Amendment) Bill, 2023
This Act talks about illegal capture and the illegal display of recorded films. Due to this imprisonment up to 3 years and a fine of 5% may be imposed.
B. Jan Vishwas Act, 2023
The Parliament passed Jan Vishwas Act of 2023 .it removes the criminal status of many offences under different acts, including the Patents Act, 1970, Copyright Act, 1957 etc. 
15. Challenges 
The owners of musical works and cinematograph films in India faces many challenges including ownership issues where a producer infringe the rights of composers and the lyricist. Sometimes the owners are not able to enforce their rights as it is complex. Further due to globalisation and piracy there arises piracy and unauthorized distribution of works.
16. Suggestions
A. owners must ensure to make contracts with composers, lyricists and other concerned parties.
B. the concerned parties must carry Out Due Diligence before starting their works related to production.
C. the owners must ensure that their films does not defeat the rights of other copyrighted works.
17. Conclusion
From the above discussion we can understand that the Supreme Court has played an important role to protect the rights of authors and producer through its landmark pronouncements. It is also our duty to ensure a system where justice is served, creativity is protected and originality is celebrated.
18. Bibliography
1. Indian Performing Rts. Soc'y Ltd. v. E. India Motion Pictures Ass'n, SC., Mar. 14, 1977, AIR, 1443,  (India), https://indiankanoon.org/doc/331232/.
2. R. G. Anand v. Deluxe Films, SC., Aug. 19, 1978, AIR, 1613, (India), https://indiankanoon.org/doc/1734007/.
3. Jyotpreet Kaur et al., A Look Back at India's Top IP Developments of 2023, SPICYIP, https://spicyip.com/2023/12/a-look-back-at-indias-top-ip-developments-of-2023.html (last visited Apr. 22, 2025).
4. Bharathwaj Ramakrishnan, A Look Back at India's Top IP Developments of 2024, SPICYIP, https://spicyip.com/2024/12/a-look-back-at-indias-top-ip-developments-of-2024.html (last visited Apr. 22, 2025).
5. MADHUSHREE CHAKRABORTY, Copyright and the Film Industry: Investigating the Legal Challenges in the Production and Distribution of Films, 12 INT'L J. CREATIVE RSCH. THOUGHTS, (2024), https://www.ijcrt.org/papers/IJCRT2401746.pdf.
